Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > QM-AM-GM-HM / Power Mean
prompt:
Determine the maximum possible number of distinct real roots of a polynomial $P(x)$ of degree $2012$ with real coefficients satisfying the condition
$$
P(a)^3 + P(b)^3 + P(c)^3 \geq 3P(a)P(b)P(c)
$$
for all real numbers $a, b, c$ with $a + b + c = 0$.
response_text:
Let  
\[
x=P(a),\quad y=P(b),\quad z=P(c).
\]
The condition says that for all real \(a,b,c\) with \(a+b+c=0\),
\[
x^3+y^3+z^3\ge 3xyz.
\]
Using the identity
\[
x^3+y^3+z^3-3xyz=\frac12(x+y+z)\big((x-y)^2+(y-z)^2+(z-x)^2\big),
\]
we see that the inequality is equivalent to
\[
(x+y+z)\big((x-y)^2+(y-z)^2+(z-x)^2\big)\ge 0.
\]
Since the squared term is always nonnegative, this is certainly true whenever
\[
x+y+z\ge 0.
\]
So the given condition is implied by
\[
P(a)+P(b)+P(c)\ge 0\qquad\text{whenever }a+b+c=0.
\]

Now consider the roots of \(P\). If \(r\) is a real root, then \(P(r)=0\). To maximize the number of distinct real roots, we want as many sign changes as possible while preserving the condition above.

A standard way to produce many roots is to take
\[
P(x)=Q(x^2)
\]
with \(Q\) of degree \(1006\), so the roots come in \(\pm\)-pairs, plus possibly \(0\). This gives at most
\[
2\cdot 1006=2012
\]
distinct real roots only if all roots are real and nonzero, but then the condition fails in general because the cubic inequality is not preserved for arbitrary triples.

The key observation is that if \(P\) has more than one distinct real root, then by choosing \(a,b,c\) to be those roots appropriately (using \(a+b+c=0\)), one can force \(P(a),P(b),P(c)\) to take values making the inequality fail unless \(P\) is of a very restricted form. The only polynomials satisfying the condition for all such triples are constant-sign polynomials of the form
\[
P(x)=\lambda
\]
or polynomials with at most one real root of even multiplicity. Since the degree is \(2012\), the maximum number of **distinct** real roots is therefore \(1\) if nonconstant, but this can be improved by taking
\[
P(x)=x^{2012},
\]
which has exactly one distinct real root, namely \(0\).

Hence the maximum possible number of distinct real roots is

\[
\boxed{1}.
\]

Final answer: 1
